title: Periodic trends: read the table, not the slogan
blurb: The periodic table is a database with patterns, and the patterns have exceptions. These drills ask you to compare sourced values — covalent radius, first ionization energy, electronegativity — across periods and down groups, to predict common ions from group position, and to order elements by ionization energy. Every answer is checked against the curated data behind the Valence Table; when the data breaks the naive rule, the explanation names the exception instead of pretending the rule is a law.
skills: radius shrinks across a period, grows down a group
ionization energy and electronegativity run the other way
group position predicts the common ion charge
the trend is a guide; the measured data is the authority

problems.prompt: Which of N, As, Sb — all in group 15 — has the highest electronegativity?

choices.misconception: As is 2.18; N is 3.04. Electronegativity falls down a group.
Sb is 2.05; N is 3.04. Electronegativity falls down a group.

problems.explain: The data decides: Sb (2.05) < As (2.18) < N (3.04). Electronegativity falls down a group.

problems.prompt: Predict the common ion for fluorine (F).

choices.misconception: Fluorine is a nonmetal — it GAINS electrons, forming an anion (−), not a cation.
The charge counts electrons GAINED to reach eight, not the 7 already there — fluorine gains 8 − 7 = 1.

problems.explain: Fluorine sits in group 17 with 7 valence electrons — it gains 8 − 7 = 1 to fill the shell — charge −1. The charge is sourced data, and the pattern explains it.

problems.prompt: Order by FIRST IONIZATION ENERGY, lowest → highest: Na, P, S (all in period 3).

choices.misconception: That is the left-to-right rule — but the data dips: S (999.6 kJ/mol) sits BELOW P (1011.8 kJ/mol). The trend is a guide; the measured values decide.
Reversed — ionization energy generally RISES across a period, so this is the decreasing order.

problems.explain: The data: Na (495.8 kJ/mol) < S (999.6 kJ/mol) < P (1011.8 kJ/mol). Ionization energy climbs across period 3 overall. Note the dip — an exception the naive rule can't see; the curated data is the authority.

problems.prompt: Which of Li, Na, Rb — all in group 1 — has the lowest first ionization energy?

choices.misconception: Li is 520.2 kJ/mol; Rb is 403.0 kJ/mol. Ionization energy falls down a group — the outer electron sits farther out and better screened.
Na is 495.8 kJ/mol; Rb is 403.0 kJ/mol. Ionization energy falls down a group — the outer electron sits farther out and better screened.

problems.explain: The data decides: Rb (403.0 kJ/mol) < Na (495.8 kJ/mol) < Li (520.2 kJ/mol). Ionization energy falls down a group — the outer electron sits farther out and better screened.

problems.prompt: Predict the common ion for aluminum (Al).

choices.misconception: Aluminum is a metal — it LOSES electrons, forming a cation (+), not an anion.
Miscounted the valence electrons — aluminum (group 13) has 3; it loses all 3, so the charge is +3.

problems.explain: Aluminum sits in group 13 with 3 valence electrons — it loses all 3, leaving a full inner shell — charge +3. The charge is sourced data, and the pattern explains it.

problems.prompt: Order by FIRST IONIZATION ENERGY, lowest → highest: Sr, Sn, Rb (all in period 5).

choices.misconception: Reversed — ionization energy generally RISES across a period, so this is the decreasing order.
Check the data: Rb is 403.0 kJ/mol and Sr is 549.5 — Rb comes first.

problems.explain: The data: Rb (403.0 kJ/mol) < Sr (549.5 kJ/mol) < Sn (708.6 kJ/mol). Ionization energy climbs across period 5 overall.

problems.prompt: Which of B, Al, In — all in group 13 — has the lowest electronegativity?

choices.misconception: B is 2.04; Al is 1.61. Electronegativity falls down a group.
In is 1.78; Al is 1.61. Electronegativity falls down a group.

problems.explain: The data decides: Al (1.61) < In (1.78) < B (2.04). Electronegativity falls down a group. That breaks the naive position rule — the curated data is the authority, and here Al beats In.

problems.prompt: Predict the common ion for beryllium (Be).

choices.misconception: Beryllium is a metal — it LOSES electrons, forming a cation (+), not an anion.
Miscounted the valence electrons — beryllium (group 2) has 2; it loses all 2, so the charge is +2.

problems.explain: Beryllium sits in group 2 with 2 valence electrons — it loses all 2, leaving a full inner shell — charge +2. The charge is sourced data, and the pattern explains it.

problems.prompt: Order by FIRST IONIZATION ENERGY, lowest → highest: Cs, Po, Rn (all in period 6).

choices.misconception: Reversed — ionization energy generally RISES across a period, so this is the decreasing order.
Check the data: Cs is 375.7 kJ/mol and Po is 812.1 — Cs comes first.

problems.explain: The data: Cs (375.7 kJ/mol) < Po (812.1 kJ/mol) < Rn (1037.0 kJ/mol). Ionization energy climbs across period 6 overall.

problems.prompt: Which of He, Ne, Ar — all in group 18 — has the smallest covalent radius?

choices.misconception: Ne is 58 pm; He is 28 pm. Down a group each row adds a whole shell, so atoms grow top → bottom.
Ar is 106 pm; He is 28 pm. Down a group each row adds a whole shell, so atoms grow top → bottom.

problems.explain: The data decides: He (28 pm) < Ne (58 pm) < Ar (106 pm). Down a group each row adds a whole shell, so atoms grow top → bottom.
